county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1851 from Sutter and Yuba counties. Named for placer mining. Auburn was a Gold Rush camp. Transcontinental Railroad crossed the Sierra here (1860s). Chinese railroad workers' history significant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Placer County Recorder in Auburn has deeds from 1851. Auburn was a Gold Rush camp that became a transportation hub. The Placer County Museum in Auburn has Gold Rush-era records. Transcontinental Railroad history is significant — Chinese workers built the Sierra Nevada crossing in the 1860s. Railroad employment records trace families. BLM mining claim records are essential. Pre-1851 records split between Sutter and Yuba counties. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the Placer Herald newspaper (from 1852) for early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Auburn — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1854 from Butte County. Named for Rio de las Plumas (Feather River). Gold mining and lumber county. Western Pacific Railroad (1909) transformed the area. Small mountain communities with limited early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Plumas County Recorder in Quincy has deeds from 1854. Pre-1854 records in Butte County. Plumas County Museum in Quincy preserves mining and logging community records. Western Pacific Railroad (completed 1909) through the Feather River Canyon generated employment records. Gold mining along the Feather River's branches drew early settlers. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. Check the Plumas National Bulletin newspaper for obituaries. BLM land patents trace homesteaders and miners. Small population makes Great Register voter rolls and school census records particularly useful for researchers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Quincy — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from San Bernardino and San Diego counties. Birthplace of California's citrus industry (Washington navel orange, 1873). March Air Reserve Base (1918). Significant Native American heritage: Cahuilla, Serrano, Luiseno peoples.
county_metadata.research_tips: Riverside County Recorder has deeds from 1893. Pre-1893 records split between San Bernardino and San Diego counties. Riverside Metropolitan Museum has extensive local history holdings. The parent Washington navel orange tree (1873) launched California's citrus industry — Riverside's citrus heritage records trace grower families. March Air Reserve Base (1918) records at NARA trace military families. Cahuilla, Serrano, and Luiseno tribal records through NARA and tribal governments. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the Riverside Press-Enterprise newspaper for obituaries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. State capital since 1854. Sutter's Fort (1839) predates the Gold Rush. Central Pacific Railroad headquarters. California State Archives and State Library are located here. Chinese community history significant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sacramento County Recorder has deeds from 1850. California State Archives and California State Library are both in Sacramento — premier genealogical resources for the entire state. Sacramento Archives and Museum Collection Center (SAMCC) holds city and county records. Sutter's Fort State Historic Park archives have records from the 1839 settlement. Central Pacific Railroad records trace railroad families. Sacramento Bee newspaper (from 1857) provides extensive obituary coverage. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records extensively. Chinese community in Sacramento dates to the Gold Rush era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1874 from Monterey County. Named for San Benito Creek. Mission San Juan Bautista (1797) is the county's cultural anchor. Agricultural county. Pre-1874 records in Monterey County.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Benito County Recorder in Hollister has deeds from 1874. Pre-1874 records in Monterey County. Mission San Juan Bautista (1797) records are essential for early families — check Archdiocese of Monterey. San Benito County Historical Society preserves local records. Agricultural community with significant Hispanic heritage from the Spanish and Mexican eras. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Hollister Free Lance newspaper provides obituaries and legal notices. Check BLM for federal land patents. Pinnacles National Park area history documented in local archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Hollister — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1853 from Los Angeles County. Largest county in the US by area. Mormon colony founded San Bernardino in 1851. Route 66 corridor. Edwards Air Force Base. Mojave Desert communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Bernardino County Recorder has deeds from 1853. The largest county by area in the United States. The Mormon colony founded San Bernardino in 1851 — LDS Church records are essential for early settlement. San Bernardino County Museum has local history collections. Route 66 heritage traces mid-20th century migration. Edwards Air Force Base and Twentynine Palms Marine Corps Base records at NARA. Pre-1853 records in Los Angeles County. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Mojave Desert mining communities generated BLM claim records. Serrano and Cahuilla tribal records through NARA.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. First European settlement in California: Mission San Diego de Alcala (1769). Presidio records date to 1769. US Navy base since 1901. Mexican border community with extensive cross-border family ties.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Diego County Recorder has records from 1850. Mission San Diego de Alcala (1769) records are the oldest European records in California — check Diocese of San Diego archives. The San Diego History Center has extensive holdings. NARA Pacific Region (Laguna Niguel) holds federal records for southern California. US Navy records at NARA trace military families stationed here since 1901. Mexican border crossing records are essential for cross-border families. FamilySearch has extensively microfilmed county records. The San Diego Union newspaper (from 1868) provides obituaries.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1906 earthquake and fire destroyed most city/county records including vital records, court records, and many property records. Some deed records survived in granite vaults.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Consolidated city-county since 1856. THE BURNED COUNTY: 1906 earthquake and fire destroyed most records. Chinese paper sons phenomenon resulted from destroyed birth records. Angel Island immigration station (1910-1940).
county_metadata.research_tips: San Francisco County records suffered catastrophic losses in the 1906 earthquake and fire. Most pre-1906 vital records, court records, and some property records were destroyed. SURVIVING RECORDS: granite vault deed records, some assessor rolls, and records stored off-site. Check the California State Library Sutro Branch (in San Francisco) for reconstructed records. National Archives (San Bruno) holds federal records that survived. Chinese Americans exploited the destroyed records through 'paper son' immigration claims — Angel Island immigration records at NARA are critical. San Francisco Public Library History Center has rebuilt collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for the San Joaquin River. Stockton was a Gold Rush supply center. Agricultural powerhouse. Filipino labor history significant. Diverse immigrant communities: Italian, Japanese, Sikh, Mexican.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Joaquin County Recorder in Stockton has deeds from 1850. Stockton was a major Gold Rush supply center and river port. San Joaquin County Historical Society and Museum has extensive local holdings. The county has remarkably diverse immigration: Italian Swiss Colony records, Japanese farming families before WWII internment, Filipino labor union records, and Sikh community records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Stockton Record newspaper provides obituaries. Check the Haggin Museum in Stockton for local history and photographs. Agricultural census records supplement federal census data.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Stockton — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa (1772) is one of the oldest in California. Hearst Castle in San Simeon. Agricultural and ranching heritage. Early Chinese fishing villages along the coast.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Luis Obispo County Recorder has records from 1850. Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa (1772) records are among the oldest in the area — check Diocese of Monterey. San Luis Obispo County Historical Society has local records. Hearst Castle (San Simeon) records at Hearst Corporation archives. Chinese fishing village records at Avila Beach document an unusual community. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The San Luis Obispo Tribune newspaper provides obituaries. Chumash tribal records through Santa Ynez Band and NARA. Rancho-era land grant records are essential for early families.
